news.title: La Liga chief claims Fifa is ‘destroying football industry’ and calls for Infantino to quit
news.lede: Javier Tebas: ‘Fifa are making irresponsible decisions’. ‘Hydration breaks are a lie … it was a break for advertising’. La Liga’s president, Javier Tebas, has accused Fifa of “destroying the football industry” and called for its president, Gianni Infantino, to quit. Tebas spoke out as the dust settled on the 2026 World Cup amid suggestions the 2030 tournament could be increased from 48 to 64 teams.

news.title: David Squires on … Spain winning the World Cup final in the shadow of Trump
news.lede: Our cartoonist looks back at the final game of the 2026 World Cup as Donald Trump watched Spain triumph again. Buy David’s cartoons | Some of his favourite works. And his latest book, Chaos in the Box: get it now.

news.title: Football, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chief | Marina Hyde
news.lede: After his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD! For so many of us around the world, the final scenes of the World Cup were a chance to switch off the TV with an eye-roll and observe mildly: “Well, I think that’s quite enough America for us all for a while now.” And yet: there can and must always be more.

news.title: Teamwork with “Tiki-Tapa”: The Secret to Spain’s World Cup Successes
news.lede: I am not an attentive soccer (football) follower, but it was hard to ignore the 2026 FIFA World Cup being played in my own country (or at least my own continent). I tuned in for more and more games as the play progressed. Spain progressed further and further along, and finally won the gold. This was not a one-off fluke.

news.title: China’s World Cup frenzy: 70 billion views, 83.3 billion yuan in lottery football sales
news.lede: The Fifa World Cup really grabbed the attention of football fans and gamblers in China, according to data from the state broadcaster and sports lottery. CCTV, the state broadcaster, shared that its tournament broadcasts, special programming and news coverage reached a cumulative cross-media audience of more than 70 billion individual views by the eve of the World Cup final. During the final between Argentina and Spain, the broadcaster revealed that live cross-media broadcasts reached a cumulative audience of 282 million, peaking with a real-time viewership of 41.23 million. It was not just casual football fans who enjoyed the World Cup – gamblers also watched with heightened tension as China’s sports lottery market experienced a surge in sales during the tournament.

news.title: World Cup becoming a 64-team tournament would create logistical challenges - so can Gianni Infantino's idea work?
news.lede: This year's World Cup is finally over - and after 48 teams took part, it was a long, long tournament. But just as we bid to say goodbye to the 2026 edition, this competition may be getting even bigger. FIFA president Gianni Infantino says any further expansion of the World Cup for 2030 - where six countries are set to host the tournament - ‌will be discussed now the 2026 World Cup has finished. And one solution FIFA are discussing is a 64-team tournament.

news.title: Football Extra's World Cup awards
news.lede: Spain captain Rodri took home Fifa's Golden Ball award for the tournament's best player – and more importantly, Football Extra's Best Player award, too. The Football Extra newsletter team pick the best players, goals and moments from the 2026 World Cup and readers have their say too. It's not too late to tell us who you think should have got a mention - email us at football.extra@bbc.com,. If you're outside the UK and would like to receive our football newsletter, sent daily Monday to Friday and covering all things football past and present, head this way.

news.title: Blame FIFA, not the USA, as $14bn 64-team World Cup angers football purists in losing battle
news.lede: That's been the case for decades and the overwhelming success of the biggest World Cup in history will only embolden Gianni Infantino in his global football push. With the USA (and Canada and Mexico) pulling off a 104-match tournament that featured 48 teams bouncing across three countries, FIFA could fast track a push for further World Cup expansion in time for the next tourney in 2030. It was initially expected that Infantino and Co. would at least hold off until a 2038 World Cup that is still waiting for a host country. "In 2030, the World Cup comes to Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay, a great opportunity for football, to celebrate the World Cup Centenary with a 64-team tournament," CONMEBOL president Alejandro Domínguez posted on social media.
